extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
58-year-old Black woman, recently widowed, formerly worked as a school district transportation dispatcher. Lived in Phoenix, AZ through early 2024, then moved to Albuquerque, NM to live closer to her adult daughter and help with grandchildren. Medicare Advantage. High health literacy but sometimes struggles with transportation, medication affordability, and keeping multiple specialist appointments aligned.

## Clinical Scenario
Denise has longstanding type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Over the past several years she also developed heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, plus chronic kidney disease with albuminuria. Her glucose control worsened during the stress of caring for her husband before his death, then she relocated out of state and had a brief gap in medication access.

In Arizona she had primary care and cardiology follow-up that established the chronic disease baseline: diabetes requiring intensification, CKD emerging, atrial fibrillation managed with anticoagulation, and HFrEF treated medically. After moving to New Mexico she had an acute decompensated heart-failure hospitalization, then longitudinal follow-up with a heart-failure clinic, nephrology, and retina specialists for diabetic retinopathy with macular edema.

## Sites
- **Desert Family Medicine** — Phoenix, AZ. Primary care, diabetes/hypertension management, preventive care, early CKD recognition.
- **Valley Heart Institute** — Phoenix, AZ. Cardiology follow-up for HFrEF and atrial fibrillation, echo review, medication optimization.
- **University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic** — Albuquerque, NM. HF hospitalization plus follow-up clinic management.
- **Rio Grande Nephrology Associates** — Albuquerque, NM. CKD stage 3b with albuminuria, BP/renal monitoring, medication safety.
- **Sandia Retina Specialists** — Albuquerque, NM. Diabetic retinopathy and macular edema evaluation, retinal imaging, intravitreal treatment.

## Clinical Threads To Exercise
- Persistent conditions: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, HFrEF, atrial fibrillation, CKD stage 3b, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, diabetic retinopathy
- Long-term meds that should persist across encounters: insulin glargine, empagliflozin, carvedilol, sacubitril/valsartan, furosemide, apixaban, atorvastatin
- Resources that should update rather than duplicate: CKD staging, HF status, atrial fibrillation management, glycemic control trends
- New acute event in the middle of the timeline: heart-failure exacerbation hospitalization after move
- Specialty-only findings/procedures: retina imaging and intravitreal injection

code.text: Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to heart-failure-related volume overload

note.text: Required supplemental oxygen on presentation for hypoxemia caused by heart-failure-related volume overload and was weaned off oxygen before discharge.

Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation remained controlled on apixaban. ECG showed sinus rhythm with occasional premature atrial complexes, and symptoms were limited to rare brief palpitations without syncope or known thromboembolism.

CKD stage 3b remained active with creatinine 1.67 mg/dL and estimated GFR about 35 mL/min/1.73 m2. Kidney function was specifically reviewed as a limiting factor for further heart-failure therapy escalation.

coding.display: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (disorder)

code.text: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

note.text: Chronic HFrEF remained stable at this follow-up with NYHA class II symptoms, stable home weights, mild exertional fatigue, trace ankle edema only, and modest NT-proBNP elevation. Renal function and potassium limited further medication escalation.
Inpatient hyperglycemia documented with serum glucose 238 mg/dL and hemoglobin A1c 8.8%. Managed with insulin glargine and correctional insulin during hospitalization.

coding.display: New York Heart Association Classification - Class II (finding)

code.text: New York Heart Association class II functional limitation due to heart failure

note.text: Functional status was reassessed at clinic follow-up and documented as NYHA class II.

resource.conclusion: Hemoglobin A1c 8.8%, consistent with suboptimal outpatient glycemic control.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: BNP markedly elevated. High-sensitivity troponin was mildly elevated but flat on repeat testing without dynamic rise.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: Chest radiograph showed pulmonary vascular congestion with small bilateral pleural effusions, supporting heart-failure-related volume overload.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: ECG demonstrated atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 122 bpm on presentation.

code.text: Basic metabolic / renal monitoring during hospitalization

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: Renal function worsened transiently during decongestion, then improved by discharge; electrolytes remained acceptable with monitoring and repletion.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram documented LVEF 33%, global left ventricular hypokinesis, mildly reduced right ventricular function, moderate left atrial enlargement, and no left ventricular thrombus.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: Chemistry results reviewed at clinic follow-up showed stable CKD stage 3b with acceptable electrolytes while continuing current heart-failure therapy.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: NT-proBNP remained elevated but improved from hospitalization, consistent with clinically improving heart failure.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: Chemistry results reviewed during cardiology follow-up showed CKD stage 3b with creatinine 1.67 mg/dL, eGFR about 35 mL/min/1.73 m2, stable electrolytes, and renal limits on further heart-failure therapy escalation.
Normal sinus rhythm at 64 bpm with left ventricular hypertrophy and occasional premature atrial complexes.

performer.display: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic

resource.conclusion: NT-proBNP remained elevated at 356 pg/mL, but the overall heart-failure assessment was clinically stable.

extension.valueMarkdown: Denise Walker presented to the University of New Mexico Hospital in Albuquerque, New Mexico and was admitted under Dr. Priya Nanduri, MD, for acute decompensated heart failure after several weeks of disrupted medication access following her move from Arizona.

extension.valueMarkdown: Denise Walker was seen at the UNM Heart Failure Clinic in Albuquerque, New Mexico by Marisol Chavez, NP, with medication review support from Kevin Romero, PharmD, 8 days after hospital discharge. Clinic vitals were BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 97.8 F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 194 lb, height 64 in, BMI 33.3 kg/m2.
